title: Politically Connected Private Equity and Employment

article.introduction:
During the 2012 presidential election, the controversy around Mitt Romney's participation in Bain Capital fuelled the debate concerning the role of private equity firms. While lawmakers and labor unions often depicted private equity firms as rent-seeking investors that make profits at the expense of target firms’ workers, others offered a different perspective.1According to aWall Street Journalarticle, for example, the White House played a crucial role during the election by encouraging the Carlyle Group, a politically connected private equity firm, to take a majority stake in a Philadelphia oil refinery, allegedly “saving 850 unionized jobs.”2Recent academic studies that reexamine the effect private equity has on employment at target firms conclude that while private equity buyouts appear to have only modest impacts on employment (Davis et al. (2014)), they positively affect target firm workers’ long-term career paths (Agrawal and Tambe (2015)). In this paper, we link the controversy about political involvement in the private equity industry to the recent literature by investigating the relation between private equity ownership, political connections, and employment.
To investigate our research question, we combine data from several sources. We first identify 3,748 targets acquired by private equity buyout firms over the 1990 to 2012 period from Capital IQ. Next, we employ the National Establishment Time-Series (NETS) database to track the employment, size, and growth of 20,073 establishments (i.e., U.S. subsidiaries, branches, and plants) operated by our sample targets, before and after the buyout. We then supplement the NETS data with target and private equity firms’ data from Capital IQ and Preqin. We also use Capital IQ to gather biographical information on the individuals affiliated with the private equity firms. We use these data to identify the political connections of each private equity firm. We classify a private equity firm as politically connected if a general partner, board member, or top employee had a major political position (e.g., served as a senator, member of Congress, governor, U.S. president, etc.) or reported an affiliation3with another person in one of those roles. Based on this definition, 14.14% of the (1,690) private equity firms in our sample are politically connected, and 33.75% (44.16%) of the target firms (establishments) in our sample are acquired by a buyout syndicate/club that includes at least one politically connected private equity firm.
Our establishment-level analysis shows that employment at existing establishments increases by 1.24% per year, on average, following a buyout by a politically connected private equity firm. By contrast, employment at existing establishments on average increases by a more modest 0.33% per year, on average, following a buyout by a nonconnected private equity firm. Thus, at least on average, buyouts by politically connected private equity firms are associated with higher job creation (or less job destruction) at the existing establishments operated by their target firms.
A caveat in the interpretation of these results, of course, relates to endogeneity. One specific concern is that buyout decisions are not random, as employment dynamics may vary across establishments and targets for reasons that are unrelated to the political connections of their private equity acquirers. For example, non–politically connected private equity firms could invest in targets that are growing at slower rates than the targets of politically connected private equity firms. To control for time-invariant establishment-specific characteristics that may be correlated with omitted explanatory variables, we exploit the panel dimension of our sample and employ fixed effects specifications. In these specifications, we compare the employment dynamics of a given establishment before and after the private equity buyout. We further augment these specifications with controls to reflect establishment and target firm attributes that might change around the time of the buyout (e.g., establishment-level size and growth, target firm leverage, tangibility, and profitability). While we recognize that we cannot control for all factors that might change around the time of the buyout, this test attempts to mitigate the sources of confounding variation.
A second endogeneity concern is that the assignment to treatment and control groups is itself not random. Politically connected private equity firms differ from their nonconnected peers on a number of dimensions, and those differences might explain the differences in the labor dynamics observed. For example, the documented differences in the labor strategies of target firms may reflect differences in local market conditions, buyout type, and/or the size of the private equity firms rather than the political connections of the private equity acquirers. To mitigate this concern, we employ a propensity score matching analysis in which we compare the employment dynamics at establishments operated by targets backed by politically connected private equity firms to those of establishments operated by similar peers backed by nonconnected private equity firms. Peers are selected from (i) the same buyout year and state if such a match exists, or (ii) the same buyout type, and then are further matched on observable establishment-level characteristics and private equity firm size. We find that establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms experience significantly higher employment growth after the buyout compared with establishments operated by otherwise similar targets. While we again acknowledge that the assignment to treatment and control groups is not random, these results mitigate the scope for alternative interpretations.
To further address selection concerns, we use placebo tests to compare realized buyouts with failed buyouts, before and after the buyout attempt. If the aforementioned results were due solely to selection, then the employment dynamics observed among targets of successful takeovers should also be observed among targets of failed takeovers. Because the decision to withdraw a takeover is itself endogenous, to understand the nature of these failed transactions and reduce remaining endogeneity concerns we focus on two specific subsamples of failed takeovers. The first subsample consists of transactions that presumably would have had a positive net present value (NPV) for the target had the deal gone through. The second subsample comprises transactions that were cancelled by the target firm or by a third party. These transactions presumably would have had a positive NPV for the private equity bidder had the transaction gone through. We do not find evidence of systematic job creation in either subsample of failed transactions subsequent to the buyout announcement; rather, the opposite is true. In addition, the difference-in-difference-in-differences (DDD) results provide evidence consistent with the idea that establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms increase employment significantly more following successful buyouts.
This evidence begs the question of why politically connected private equity firms boost employment at their targets. Perhaps some private equity firms boost employment to maintain good public relations and/or good relations with the government. Private equity principals and/or politicians might also boost employment to promote their own social capital and career concerns. It could also be the case that the employment growth documented among the targets of politically connected private equity firms is part of an exchange of favors (quid pro quo) between politicians and firms.4
The data allow us to investigate this third explanation. We find three pieces of evidence that are consistent with this exchange of favors story. First, we document that, among the targets of politically connected private equity firms, the observed increase in employment is more pronounced among private equity investments during election years and in states with high levels of corruption, that is, when and where we might expect the incentives for exchanges of favors to be greatest. Second, we document that increases in employment (at the state level) are positively associated with the likelihood that the incumbent will be reelected—indicating one source of gains for the politicians. Third, we identify specific benefits that target firms receive from their political connections, namely the award of government contracts and grants.
Our paper contributes to the growing literature on corporate political connections. We provide evidence about some specific channels (costs and revenues) through which political connections might affect the value of private equity targets in the United States. The paper also contributes to the literature on the impact of private equity ownership on employment at buyout targets. Specifically, we document that on average targets of politically connected private equity buyout firms increase employment at their existing establishments following a buyout.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionI.discusses related literature. SectionII.describes the data and identification of politically connected private equity firms. SectionIII.presents results of the main analyses as well as additional analyses designed to mitigate endogeneity concerns. SectionIV.investigates the exchange of favors story. SectionV.presents various robustness tests. SectionVI.concludes.
article.I. Related Literature:
This paper relates to the literature on political connections. In this literature, prior studies find widespread evidence that, at least on average, political connections positively affect firm value. Many studies investigate the benefits that politically connected firms realize, including preferential access to credit (Cull and Xu (2005), Khwaja and Mian (2005), Houston et al. (2014)), lighter taxation (De Soto (1989)), government contracts (Goldman, Rocholl, and So (2013)), government aid (Faccio, Masulis, and McConnell (2005)), relaxed regulatory oversight (Stigler (1971), De Soto (1989), Kroszner and Stratmann (1998)), a lower likelihood of fraud detection (Yu and Yu (2011)), and less severe enforcement outcomes (Fulmer and Knill (2012)). There is less evidence, however, about the benefits that politicians receive from companies. Presumably, as modeled by Shleifer and Vishny (1994), politicians provide aid to firms in exchange for personal benefits such as votes or cash payments. Along these lines, Bertrand et al. (2007) document that French firms connected to politicians create more jobs, especially around election years and in more contested regions.
In this paper, we provide novel evidence about some of the channels through which political connections may affect the value of private equity targets in the United States. Our analysis investigates job creation at buyout targets as one cost to the firm with political connections, and examines government contracts (and grants) as well as industrial revenue bonds (IRBs) as two possible benefits.
The paper connects the discussion about political involvement in the private equity industry to recent literature investigating the employment consequences of private equity ownership.5One view holds that private equity, and in particular leveraged buyouts, destroy jobs. For example, anecdotal evidence suggests that private equity investors “sucked the lifeblood of GST Steel … destroying jobs and eliminating pensions.”6Earlier academic studies provide support for this claim, although they disagree on the magnitude of the effect.7For example, using a sample of 76 large buyouts completed over the 1980 to 1986 period, Kaplan (1989) documents that the median firm reduced employment by as much as 12% (on an industry-adjusted basis) around the year of the buyout. Amess and Wright (2007), Lichtenberg and Siegel (1990), and Wright, Thompson, and Robbie (1992), however, show that buyouts are followed by more modest declines in employment. More recently, Davis et al. (2014), who examine 3,600 leveraged buyouts using U.S. Census data, find that, although private equity is associated with more job losses at existing business locations, it creates more jobs at new establishments. They conclude that “private equity buyouts catalyze the creative destruction process … with only a modest net impact on employment.”
In this paper, we document a source of heterogeneity in the behavior of private equity firms and shed new light on the debate about the impact of private equity on employment. We document that establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity buyout firms marginally increase employment following a buyout.
article.II. Data:
Information on private equity firms and their buyout targets comes from Capital IQ. From this database, we identify a sample of private equity targets using the following criteria: (1) the target firm is located in the United States; (2) the transaction is completed between 1990 and 2012; and (3) the acquirer includes (at least) one investment firm that has a reported investment interest in one of the following stages: bridge, buyout, early venture, emerging growth, growth capital, incubation, industry consolidation, late venture, mature, mezzanine/subdebt, middle market, midventure, pipes, seed/startup, or turnaround. To identify buyout targets, we follow Axelson et al. (2013) and select transactions classified as “leveraged buyout,” “management buyout,” or “going private transactions.” We focus on buyouts because those transactions have been most heavily criticized for “destroying” jobs. This search yields an initial sample of 6,989 private equity targets.
We gather employment data from the NETS longitudinal database constructed by Walls & Associates using Dun and Bradstreet (D&B) data. The NETS database provides annual employment and sales data for more than 52 million U.S. businesses and establishments (i.e., headquarters, subsidiaries, branches, and plants across the United States). This database maintains an essentially complete record of all establishments going back to 1989. Establishments are not legally required to report to D&B; however, D&B is a leading provider of business credit information and thus those establishments that wish to obtain lines of credit with suppliers or financial institutions have incentives to report to D&B. Additionally, D&B attempts to develop complete business lists by collecting information from independent sources, including phone calls, legal and bankruptcy filings, press reports, payment and collection activities, and government and postal records. Recent studies employing NETS data include Levine and Toffel (2010), Neumark, Wall, and Zhang (2011), Levine, Toffel, and Johnson (2012), Chang and Schoar (2013), and Heider and Ljungqvist (2015).
Using establishment-level data has a number of advantages. First, we are able to measure organic employment growth. Second, we are able to disentangle organic employment growth from sales and acquisitions of plants, which do not result in job losses or gains if employees keep their jobs under the new ownership. Third, the data allow us to investigate job creation at newly opened establishments and job destruction at plants that are closed.
We match the sample of private equity targets identified from Capital IQ with NETS based on the name and address of each target company's headquarters. We collect data for all establishments that belong to the headquarters that match our private equity targets. We track employment for up to five years after the private equity transaction. We truncate the sample five years after the initial private equity investment, as this represents the typical holding period of private equity funds (Strömberg (2007)).8Establishments enter the sample for the first time at the first year-end they appear in NETS as operated by a given firm that eventually becomes a buyout target. Establishments purchased by target firms are included in the analysis starting the first year-end after the acquisition. We discontinue coverage of establishments divested when their ownership changes hands. Thus, by construction, our approach does not treat changes in employment associated with the acquisition or divestiture of establishments as job creation or destruction. (We investigate plant openings and closures in SectionV..)
Our procedure matches 54% of the 6,989 private equity targets in Capital IQ to the NETS database, yielding a final sample of 3,748 private equity targets operating 20,073 establishments as of the transaction year and 255,355 target establishment-year observations. For each establishment, we obtain annual employment, employment growth, sales, and sales growth data from NETS. We obtain data on the buyout type (e.g., management buyout, going private transaction, etc.) from Capital IQ. Additional firm-level accounting data for the private equity targets, including the ratio of taxes over earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (EBITDA), leverage ratios, tangibility ratios, cash ratios, return on assets, and profit margins, when available, come from Capital IQ. Those additional firm-level accounting data are available only for a limited set of targets that are required to (or chose to) report data after the buyout (i.e., 344 of 3,748 target firms). Private equity firm data come from Capital IQ and Preqin. To reduce the impact of outliers, in the analyses that follow, we winsorize all continuous variables from NETS and Capital IQ at the 1% level.
From Capital IQ, we extract biographical information for each individual in the 1,690 private equity firms that invest in our sample of 3,748 targets to determine whether these private equity firms are politically connected. These individuals are primarily the private equity firms’ (general) partners, directors, vice-presidents, and top employees (such as associates and principals). Each biography includes detailed information about the individual's education, professional experience, and political positions and affiliations. We use the keywords in the Appendix to identify political positions and affiliations. These keywords include “Governor of the State,” “Senator,” “Congress,” “White House,” “President Carter,” “President Reagan,” “President Bush,” “President Clinton,” as well as each agency in the Cabinet. We define a private equity firm as politically connected if at least one individual in the private equity firm served in one of those political positions or has a reported affiliation with another person in one of those roles at any time during the sample period. (We thus treat political connections as time invariant at the private equity firm level.)
For example, we classify private equity firm Kohlberg Kravis Roberts as politically connected as Kenneth B. Mehlman, Head of Global Public Affairs at Kohlberg Kravis Roberts, is a former Chairman of the Republican National Committee and President Bush's Election Campaign Manager. Similarly, we classify Forstmann Little & Co. and the Fortress Investment Group LLC as politically connected as Colin L. Powell, a member of the advisory board of Forstmann Little & Co., is the 65thU.S. Secretary of State, and John Edwards, a Senior Advisor of the Fortress Investment Group LLC, is a former Vice Presidential candidate and a U.S. Senator. We integrate the data in Capital IQ with news articles. While a large number of articles mention political connections in the private equity industry, news searches allow us to identify only one additional politically connected private equity firm that engaged in three buyouts.
Note that political affiliations represent instances in which the individual in question worked for a politician or a government agency, as opposed to holding a political office herself. School ties and possible relationships stemming from living in the same neighborhood as a politician are not treated as affiliations—only current or past work experience is considered. Examples of political affiliations include (the following quotes are from the bios as reported in Capital IQ) Anya Hoerburger, Director of Investor Relations at InterMedia Advisors LLC, who “served as a Finance Director at U.S. Senator Tom Daschle's reelection campaign” and Ward B. Nickisch, Administrative Partner at Development Capital Ventures, who “served as a senior member on United States delegations to international negotiations and has coordinated a variety of sensitive activities with the Department of Defense, the Department of State, and the Central Intelligence Agency.” Political affiliations represent a minority of the connections in the sample.
In our sample, 14.14% of the private equity firms are classified as politically connected. Among our sample of private equity targets, 1,265 (33.75%) are backed by at least one private equity firm that is politically connected.9
In later analyses, we employ data from additional sources. In this section, we briefly introduce these sources. Failed takeovers are identified from Capital IQ and the Securities Data Company (SDC) “Mergers & Acquisitions” database. For these takeovers, we conduct comprehensive searches of information in Capital IQ and SDC and news articles in Factiva and LexisNexis to determine the reason(s) for the failure of the transaction, along with information on the offer price, the price of any competing offers, and the stock price of the target firm prior to the buyout announcement.
To investigate the exchange of favors story, we gather data on presidential elections and gubernatorial elections (including the year of the election and the electoral outcome, by state) from both Dave Leip's Atlas of U.S. Presidential Elections and Wikipedia. State-level unemployment rates come from the U.S. Department of Labor (Bureau of Labor Statistics). State-level per capita personal income data are from the U.S. Department of Commerce (Bureau of Economic Analysis). Data on state-level corruption are fromThe Report to Congress on the Activities and Operations of the Public Integrity Section. Swing states are identified based on Gerber et al. (2009).
With regard to the benefits that target firms experience, data on government contracts (and grants) come from NETS. Data on IRBs come from Mergent's Municipal Bond Securities Database.
article.III. Employment Dynamics:
In Panel A of TableI, we provide univariate results and descriptive statistics for the establishments operated by targets of politically connected and nonconnected private equity firms. During thepre-buyout period, the average annual change in employment for establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms is 1.98%, while for establishments operated by targets of nonconnected private equity firms this figure is 1.58%. The difference is statistically and economically insignificant. Following a buyout, the average annual change in employment for establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms declines to 1.24%, but the average annual change in employment for establishments operated by targets of nonconnected private equity firms drops even further, to 0.33%. The difference between the two is statistically significant with ap-value of less than 0.001. Thus, following a buyout, establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms experience a significantly higher rate of employment growth.
In Panel A of TableI, we also summarize other observable establishment-level characteristics. These characteristics include employment levels, sales, sales growth, private equity firm size (the cumulative size of funds invested by private equity firms as of the buyout year), and the type of buyout such as management buyout, going-private transactions, and all other buyout types (typically leveraged buyouts). The table shows that establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms are more likely to be acquired by larger private equity firms and are more likely to be targets of management buyouts and/or going-private transactions compared to establishments operated by targets of nonconnected private equity firms.
In Panel B of TableI, we compare cross-sectional (target-level) accounting variables for 164 targets of politically connected private equity firms and 180 targets of nonconnected private equity firms with available pre- and post-buyout accounting data provided by Capital IQ. Targets of politically connected private equity firms are larger in size (measured by the book value of assets) but hold less cash before the buyout, while they show a higher profit margin than their nonconnected counterparts after the buyout. Other target-level observable characteristics are statistically similar between the two groups.
In our analyses, selection issues represent an important consideration. The univariate tests above show that establishments operated by targets of politically connected and nonconnected private equity firms differ across dimensions that may correlate with their labor policies. The private equity acquirers also differ across dimensions that may correlate with the labor policies of their target firms. We attempt to mitigate these concerns using three approaches.
In TableII, we present, Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) panel regressions of the relations among private equity, political connections, and employment. The panel dimension of our database allows us to include establishment-level fixed effects to control for time-invariant establishment-level characteristics, such as geography, that may be correlated with omitted variables. We can thus compare the employment dynamics of the same establishments before and after the private equity buyout. The inclusion of establishment-level fixed effects mitigates the concern that politically connected private equity firms may select targets whose labor policies differ from the targets of nonconnected private equity firms for reasons unrelated to the private equity acquirers’ political connections. We cluster the standard errors at the target firm level to account for the correlation of the residuals within a firm.
To further mitigate the sources of confounding variation, we augment all specifications with controls to reflect time-varying establishment attributes that might change around the time of a buyout. Specifically, the richness of the NETS data enables us to control for the size of each establishment (as proxied by its sales), sales growth, and lagged employment. As expected, in regression (1) we find that establishments experiencing a growth in revenues increase employment while establishments that are shrinking are associated with higher rates of job destruction. Also as expected, employment grows at a faster rate among establishments with a lower initial number of employees.
Regression (1) also includes aPost-PEindicator that takes the value of one if the observation year is after an investment by a private equity firm (as reported in Capital IQ) and zero otherwise. The coefficient on thePost-PEindicator captures the change in employment following the private equity investment relative to the employment change before the investment. Among the establishments operated by the targets of non–politically connected private equity firms, the rate of growth in employment (reported in regression (1)) declines by a marginal 0.4 percentage points after the buyout.
We also interact thePost-PEindicator with aPolitically Connectedindicator, which takes the value of one if at least one of the target firm's private equity investors is politically connected (as defined above) and zero otherwise.10This interaction reflects differences in the post-buyout employment growth rates of establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms as compared to establishments operated by targets of nonconnected private equity firms. The positive and significant sign on the interaction term indicates that the decline in the employment growth rate is significantly less pronounced among targets of politically connected private equity firms.
As the univariate results show, targets of connected and nonconnected private equity firms differ from targets of nonconnected private equity firms on a number of dimensions. We therefore add controls to account for these characteristics. For this purpose, we supplement the NETS data with target-level data from Capital IQ. Doing so enables us to include additional controls for the target's effective tax rate, leverage, tangibility, cash holdings, and profitability in regression (2). The resulting sample of approximately 17,000 of 255,355 establishment-year observations is less complete, however, and itself presents selection issues. For example, it primarily includes establishments operated by targets that continue to make disclosures to the SEC after the buyout, that is, the subset of targets that remain publicly traded after the buyout. With these caveats in mind, the results in regression (2) nevertheless corroborate the evidence in regression (1) for the larger and more inclusive sample of all targets in NETS. In particular, the results confirm that employmentgrows more, on average, following buyouts by politically connected private equity firms. In this sense, politically connected private equity buyout firms do not appear to destroy jobs.
Of course, our conclusions are subject to the caveat that the targets of politically connected and nonconnected private equity firms might differ on other dimensions, including those that are unobservable to us due to data constraints. Thus, with this test we can only mitigate the sources of confounding variation.
A second concern is that the assignment to treatment and control groups is not random—that is, buyouts by politically connected private equity firms differ from their nonconnected peers along a number of dimensions, including buyout year, buyout state, private equity firms’ size, and private equity firms’ investment preferences on the type of buyout—and that those differences might explain the differences in labor dynamics observed. To mitigate this concern, we employ a propensity score matching analysis of establishments operated by buyout targets. We compare the pre-buyout labor dynamics of establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms to those of establishments operated by targets of nonconnected private equity firms that display similar observable private equity firm and establishment-level characteristics. By matching deals attempted in the same year and region (i.e., state), we mitigate the concern that the documented differences reflect local market conditions. Alternatively, by matching establishments based on acquirer characteristics, we mitigate the concern that the documented differences reflect acquirer attributes that correlate with the private equity firms’ political connectedness.
For this purpose, we employ the propensity score matching procedure of Rosenbaum and Rubin (1983). This procedure allows us to identify a control group of targets of nonconnected private equity firms that exhibit minimalobservabledifferences in attributes in comparison with the targets of politically connected private equity firms. We then compare post-buyout employment growth between the two groups. As the control firms are virtually indistinguishable in terms of observable characteristics, the targets of politically connected private equity firms are expected to exhibit the same post-private equity investment employment growth patterns as the targets of nonconnected private equity firms.
In Panel A of TableIII, we match deals attempted in the same year and region (i.e., state) as a function of private equity firm size, along with the time series mean of pre-buyout establishment-level employment growth, the natural log of employment, sales growth, and the natural log of sales. (The sample size considerations discussed earlier prevent us from matching on target firm characteristics.) To do so, we compute the probability that an establishment with given characteristics is targeted by a politically connected private equity firm. To ensure that the establishments in the control group are sufficiently similar to the establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms, we require that the maximum difference between the propensity score of the targets of politically connected private equity firms and the propensity score of matching peers does not exceed 0.5% in absolute value.11Given this constraint, we find a match for 535 of the 8,864 establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms.
As a reference, in Panel A of TableIII, we report the mean of the time series means of annual pre-buyout characteristics for the establishments operated by the targets of politically connected private equity firms and for the establishments operated by the targets of nonconnected private equity firms. With the exception of the pre-buyout level of employment and private equity firm size, after matching there are no major differences in the pre-buyout characteristics of the establishments operated by targets of politically connected and nonconnected firms. For example, the two groups are similar in terms of pre-buyout employment growth, sales growth, and size. More important, the results in TableIIIcorroborate our earlier evidence that, following private equity investment, employment increases significantly more in establishments operated by the targets of politically connected private equity firms than in control group establishments operated by the targets of nonconnected private equity firms.
In Panel B of TableIII, we match establishments within each buyout type (i.e., management buyout, going-private transaction, or other buyout type, which includes leveraged buyouts) based on private equity firm size as well as the time series mean of pre-buyout employment growth, the natural log of employment, sales growth, the natural log of sales, and establishment-level two-digit SIC codes (the list of two-digit SIC codes used in this table is reported in Table IA.I in the Internet Appendix12). As Panel B of TableIIIshows, our conclusions are robust to matching using these different criteria. We recognize, of course, that while the matching procedures control for selection on observable fund and firm characteristics, identification will still be compromised to the extent that there remain unobservable differences between the two sets of targets that correlate with the political connections of the private equity acquirers.
The analyses in the previous sections can control only for differences in observable private equity acquirer, target firm, and establishment-level characteristics. Further, the results above may also obtain if different types of private equity firms aretargetingfirms with different job dynamics or other systematicunobservablecharacteristics, instead of actuallyaffectingjob dynamics. For example, politically connected private equity firms might specialize in deals requiring less employment downsizing post-transaction. This story is ex ante plausible because connected private equity firms have invested heavily in political capital and thus may want to avoid deals with more negative political implications.
To mitigate this concern, we compare realized buyouts with failed buyouts, both before and after the transaction attempt. More precisely, we extend Seru (2014) and use a sample that pools realized buyouts with failed buyouts (by politically connected and nonconnected private equity firms). We employ this methodology to help generate exogenous variation in takeover outcomes. If the differences in job dynamics documented earlier would have happened anyway, then they should also be present among targets of failed takeovers. In other words, a simple selection story predicts that employment should increase (decrease) among targets of politically connected (nonconnected) private equity firms even if the takeover fails.
To investigate this possibility, we identify failed takeovers from Capital IQ using the following criteria: (1) the target firm is located in the United States; (2) the acquirer includes at least one investment firm with a reported investment interest in one of the following stages: bridge, buyout, early venture, emerging growth, growth capital, incubation, industry consolidation, late venture, mature, mezzanine/subdebt, middle market, mid-venture, pipes, seed/startup, or turnaround; (3) the transaction is classified as a “leveraged buyout,” “management buyout,” or “going-private transaction”; and (4) the transaction was withdrawn over the 1990 to 2012 period. We supplement the sample of failed takeovers using the SDC Mergers & Acquisitions database, from which we identify additional private equity transactions cancelled between 1990 and 2012 and that are backed by any private equity firm in Capital IQ. Matching the targets of failed takeover attempts from Capital IQ and SDC to the NETS database yields a final sample of failed takeover attempts that includes 7,126 establishments operated by 134 private equity targets, of which 2,161 (30.33%) are operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms.
Different from Seru (2014), the initial comparison of the characteristics of the establishments operated by targets of failed and successful takeovers (the results can be found in Table IA.II in the Internet Appendix) indicates that failed and successful buyouts differ significantly on multiple dimensions. As identification comes from the establishments operated by targets of unsuccessful buyouts acting as a counterfactual for how the establishments operated by successful targets would have increased employment after the buyout (had they not been acquired), using matched samples therefore becomes imperative. To reduce the sources of confounding variation, we employ a propensity score matching approach, where we match successful and failed buyouts. We then use a regression approach to compare the labor dynamics of failed takeovers with those of successful takeovers that share similar characteristics (i.e., the propensity score matched sample). Specifically, in the propensity score matching, we control for private equity firm size, the time series mean of pre-buyout employment growth, the natural log of employment, sales growth, and the natural log of sales, as well as for buyout attempt year and state fixed effects to account for local market conditions.13With these constraints, we find a match for 2,969 of the 7,126 establishments operated by 70 targets of failed takeover attempts. The matching sample includes 2,969 establishments operated by 452 targets of successful buyouts. As the results in Panel A of TableIVindicate, the pre-buyout characteristics of establishments operated by failed and successful takeover targets do not generally differ significantly after the matching.
Panel D of TableIVpresents DDD regressions of the impact of takeover success on employment dynamics at establishments operated by targets of connected versus establishments operated by the targets of nonconnected private equity firms. Standard errors are once again clustered at the target firm level. The results in column (1) are based on the propensity score matched samples from Panel A. A caveat in interpreting the results in column (1) is that the sample includes both buyouts terminated for exogenous reasons and buyouts terminated for endogenous reasons. The dependent variable is the annual rate of establishment-level employment growth. As regression (1) shows, the coefficient on the triple interaction is positive, although not statistically significant at conventional levels. The positive sign on the triple interaction term indicates that the decline in the employment growth rate is marginally less pronounced among establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms, especially so for targets of successful buyouts. The identifying assumption of this test is that deals across politically connected and nonpolitically connected firms fail (or succeed) for similar reasons, and hence any differences between realized and failed deals across connected and nonconnected firms are due to the political connections.
We recognize that this assumption may be too strong. Admittedly, the decision to withdraw a takeover is itself endogenous. Therefore, to further understand the nature of the failed takeovers and reduce remaining endogeneity concerns, we further examine two sets of takeovers that failed for presumably “exogenous” reasons. In particular, we focus on (1) transactions that would have generated a positive NPV for the target had the deal gone through, and (2) transactions that are terminated by the target firm or by a third party, such as the Department of Justice or a bankruptcy court.14
The first sample of buyouts that failed for “exogenous” reasons includes buyouts in which the price offered by the losing bidder (which includes a private equity firm) is (1) higher than the price offered by the winning bidder (when there is a competing offer) or (2) higher than the target firm's stock price on the day prior to the announcement of the buyout attempt (when there is no competing offer). We use deal descriptions in Capital IQ and SDC as well as comprehensive news searches in Factiva and LexisNexis to gather information on the pricing of the failed buyout attempts. Matching the targets of failed takeover attempts from Capital IQ and SDC to the NETS database yields a sample of failed takeover attempts with presumably positive NPVs (for the targets) that includes 414 establishments operated by 25 private equity targets.
As in Panel A of TableIV, we use a propensity score matching approach to match the establishments operated by the targets of failed takeover attempts with the establishments of successful takeovers that display similar pre-buyout establishment-level characteristics (including employment growth). We further include controls for attempt-year and state. With these constraints, we find a match for 207 of the 414 establishments operated by 11 targets of failed takeover attempts. The matching sample includes 207 establishments operated by 54 targets of successful buyouts.
A comparison of the characteristics of these matched samples is provided in Panel B of TableIV. The comparison indicates that successful and failed buyouts are statistically similar in every dimension. Regression (2) in Panel D of TableIVpresents the DDD regression results for the matched sample. As the results show, the coefficient on the triple interaction is positive and significant. This result supports the idea that the differences in the employment dynamics observed across establishments operated by targets of connected and nonconnected private equity firms are unlikely to be entirely due to differences in private equity firms’ selection technologies.
We also consider a second sample of buyouts that failed for “exogenous” reasons. This sample includes deals that were cancelled by the buyout target or by a third party. Those transactions are identified based on information reported in Capital IQ and SDC, along with news searches in Factiva and LexisNexis. The searches result in a sample of failed takeover attempts, with presumably positive NPV for the private equity firms, that includes 337 establishments from 22 failed takeovers. Using the propensity score matching algorithm described above, we find a match for 100 (of the 337) establishments operated by 13 targets of failed takeover attempts. The matching sample includes 100 establishments operated by 73 targets of successful buyouts.
The DDD results for this matched sample are reported in column (3) of TableIV, Panel D. The coefficient on the triple interaction is again positive and significant. Overall, comparing different samples of failed and successful private equity transactions that were terminated for presumably exogenous reasons, we conclude that the employment dynamics we document appear to be consistent with the idea that politically connected private equity firms actively create jobs at their target firms following a successful takeover.15,16
article.IV. Exchanges of Favors between Private:
The evidence above begs the question of why politically connected private equity firms boost employment at their targets. One possibility is that politically connected private equity firms intentionally alter the hiring decisions of their targets to curry favor with their political allies in an attempt to obtain future benefits in return. To investigate the plausibility of this exchange of favors explanation, we first look at employment cycles during times and/or at locations where the potential for an exchange of favors is greatest. The exchange of favors story predicts that employment growth will be more pronounced in (1) election years, (2) more corrupt states, and/or (3) swing states. In TableV, we test these predictions. We focus on election years, corruption, and electoral uncertainty in the state in which a given establishment is located. As the key variables of interest are measured at the state level, in TableVwe cluster the standard errors at the state level to account for the correlation of the residuals. To account for the relatively small number of clusters (N= 50), we use the wild bootstrap approach developed in Cameron, Gelbach, and Miller (2008) to compute the clustered standard errors.
We begin by separating the observation years into “election years” and “nonelection years.” Election years consist of years in which a presidential election (Federal Election Year) or a gubernatorial election (State Election Year) takes place. We obtain gubernatorial election years from Dave Leip's Atlas of U.S. Presidential Elections and Wikipedia.17The results are reported in regressions (1) and (2), respectively. As election years might coincide with economic cycles, in the models we control for state-level per capita Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and GDP growth. The results show that the growth in employment observed among the establishments operated by the targets of politically connected private equity firms is more pronounced (relative to the establishments operated by the targets of nonconnected private equity firms) for transactions that occurred in gubernatorial election years (p-value = 0.08).
The exchange of favors story also predicts that the increase in employment experienced by the establishments operated by the targets of politically connected private equity firms is more pronounced in more corrupt states. We therefore investigate whether differences in the level of corruption in the state in which an establishment is based are associated with differences in employment dynamics. We use the number of public corruption convictions from 2001 to 2010 divided by the state population in 2010 as a proxy for state-level corruption.18This measure has been widely used in the literature on corruption (e.g., Meier and Holbrook (1992), Goel and Nelson (1998)). We classify states with an above-median corruption index as corrupt. Regression (3) shows that post-buyout employment growth among the establishments operated by the targets of politically connected private equity firms is significantly higher in more corrupt states. These results further reinforce the quid pro quo story.
Further, the incentive to hire (or retain) employees to buy votes presumably decreases when an election's outcome is predictable. Instead, politicians will care more about marginal votes when an election race is close. Thus, if an exchange of favors story explains our results, the employment growth experienced by targets of politically connected private equity firms should be more pronounced in swing states. To identify swing states, we rely on the frequency with which Gerber et al. (2009) classify a state as “pivotal” in a presidential election between 1972 and 2008. We classify states with an above median value as swing states.19In regression (4) of TableV, we investigate whether employment dynamics differ in swing states. We find that post-buyout employment growth among establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms is more pronounced in swing states, although insignificantly so. In regression (5), we include all interaction terms. The results are broadly consistent with the findings in regressions (1) to (4).
To establish the incentives for politicians to exchange favors with firms, we investigate whether voters respond to employment conditions when deciding whether to reelect the incumbent party. For this purpose, we continue to focus on gubernatorial and presidential elections during the 1990 to 2012 period. In TableVI, the dependent variable is the change in the fraction of votes obtained by the incumbent party between the current and the previous elections. Electoral outcomes are measured at the state level. Standard errors are therefore clustered at the state level. Once again, given the presence of a relatively small number of clusters, we employ the wild bootstrap approach developed in Cameron, Gelbach, and Miller (2008).
The independent variable of interest is the change in the state-level employment rate (i.e., one minus the unemployment rate) between the year of the current election and the year of the previous election. Regressions (1) to (4) report the results for the gubernatorial elections; regressions (5) to (8) report the results for the presidential elections. To mitigate omitted variable concerns, in the models we progressively add controls for (per capita) GDP growth (since the previous election), year fixed effects, and state fixed effects.
For both sets of elections, the results indicate that increases in the level of employment (at the state level) are associated with a higher likelihood that the incumbent will be reelected—suggesting one source of gain for politicians. These results are consistent with earlier evidence in Nordhaus (1975), Rogoff (1990), and Bertrand et al. (2007), among others, and with the idea that incumbent politicians should care about local employment conditions when going up for reelection.
Finally, we provide evidence on the incentives for firms to change their employment decisions to help the incumbent politicians remain in power. One explanation is that firms grant such employment favors in exchange for economic advantages. The data allow us to investigate two such sources of benefits: the allocation of procurement contracts and low interest loans (e.g., IRBs). Data on government contracts (and grants) come from NETS, while data on IRBs are from Mergent's Municipal Bond Securities Database. IRBs are securities issued by public entities (mostly municipalities) to provide cheap financing to firms. Specifically, for IRBs that meet certain criteria, interest is tax-exempt at the investor level.20Because of this exemption, the bonds presumably carry a lower interest rate, which represents a benefit to the firms.
In TableVII, we report conditional (i.e., fixed effects) logistic regressions of the benefits received by the establishments operated by private equity targets. In TableVII, the standard errors are clustered at the target firm level. We find that, post-buyout, establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms are disproportionately more likely to receive government contracts (and grants). We also find some evidence consistent with a higher likelihood of getting preferential access to IRBs for establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms. The IRB results, however, lack statistical significance when we cluster the standard errors at the target firm level. Nevertheless, the evidence in this section is largely consistent with a quid pro quo story.
article.V. Robustness Tests:
In this section, we conduct additional analyses to assess the robustness of the main results. Thus far we have focused on the “organic” growth in establishments acquired as part of the initial buyout. By construction, this analysis misses job creation at new establishments that are opened following a buyout. A concern would arise if targets of politically connected private equity firms were less likely to open new establishments. To investigate this possibility, in TableVIII, regressions (1) and (2), we present results of negative binomial (fixed effects) regressions of establishment openings by each target firm. The dependent variable is the count of newly opened establishments in each year by each private equity target. We find that targets of politically connected private equity firms are more likely to open new establishments after a buyout. This result complements our previous findings of job creation.
For completeness, we also investigate job destruction at shuttered establishments. Regressions (3) and (4) of TableVIIIreport the results. Interestingly, we find that targets of politically connected private equity firms are less likely to shut down establishments following a buyout relative to their nonconnected peers, although insignificantly so. These results reinforce our earlier evidence of more pronounced organic job creation at existing establishments following a buyout by politically connected private equity firms.
So far, the analyses have focused mainly on the rate of employmentgrowth. TableIXreports panel regressions with thelevelof employment as the dependent variable. Consistent with our previous findings for employment growth, employmentlevelsalso increase among targets of politically connected private equity firms after the buyout (regressions (1) and (2)). Our initial findings are therefore also robust to using the level of employment as the dependent variable.
We next investigate the robustness of the results to modifying our measure of political connectedness. In particular, we alternatively employ the fraction of politically connected employees for each private equity buyout club (as opposed to using an indicator variable). When we rerun our main regressions of TableII(the results are not reported here to save space; they can be found in Table IA.V in the Internet Appendix), we find that our main results are robust to using this alternative measure of connectedness. Further, in unreported tests we find that the type of connection matters as well. In particular, connections with federal officials (e.g., members of the Cabinet and members of Congress) appear to matter more for post-takeover employment outcomes than connections with state officials (e.g., governors). We report these results in Table IA.VI in the Internet Appendix.
We also investigate whether the results are specific to one type of buyout. To do so, we run separate regressions for management buyouts, going-private transactions, and all other buyouts. Across all types of buyouts, we find less job destruction (or more job creation) among the targets of politically connected private equity firms. In economic terms, we find the results to be strongest for leveraged buyouts. These results can be found in Table IA.VI in the Internet Appendix.
Last but not least, we split the sample into pre- and post-2000 groups. In unreported tests, we find that the “effect” of private equity political connection on post-buyout employment is specific to the most recent wave of buyouts in the 2000s. We report the results in Table IA.VI in the Internet Appendix.
article.VI. Conclusions:
In this paper, we investigate the employment dynamics among establishments operated by targets of private equity buyout firms. In contrast to widespread criticisms of private equity, we document that establishments operated by targets of politically connected private equity firms significantly boost employment following a buyout.
We employ various econometric techniques to mitigate selection concerns. For example, placebo tests that use buyouts that failed for presumably exogenous reasons as counterfactuals suggest that the success of a buyout appears to affect employment dynamics at establishments operated by targets of successful buyouts (by politically connected private equity firms). However, absent a clear source of indisputably exogenous variation, our evidence can be interpreted as only suggestive of a causal relation.
The results appear to be consistent with an exchange of favors story. In particular, the evidence is consistent with politically connected private equity firms altering their hiring and firing decisions to support the election of their political allies. Indeed, we show that the likelihood that incumbent politicians get reelected is highly influenced by local employment dynamics. Further, we document specific benefits that accrue to the establishments operated by target firms. Thus, the political cycle story documented for France by Bertrand et al. (2007) appears to extend to the United States, and to the private equity industry. After considering the employment consequences of private equity buyouts, some of the earlier conclusions and criticisms about this industry may need to be reconsidered.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
